text: The surgeon begins an emergency diagnostic laparoscopy, confirming severe small bowel distension after optical entrance with a blunt trocar in the left upper quadrant. The suspected ileocecal valve cancer is visually confirmed. Adhesions from a previous open collar cystectomy elevate the right colon towards the abdominal wall. The surgeon uses a thirty-degree scope and maximum rotation of the operating bed to navigate the challenging view caused by extremely dilated and frail bowel loops, which are carefully mobilized from the mesentery.

The surgeon carefully mobilizes the small bowel loops to create a working space, exposing the ileocecal mesentery. The adhesions between the right colon and the anterior abdominal wall aid in this exposure. The ileocolic vessels are dissected, ligated, and sectioned. The thin and unfolded right mesocolon is noted. The duodenum and iliac artery are visualized, and a medial to lateral mobilization of the right mesocolon is performed, with the right ureter also being visualized.

The surgeon dissects the distal mesentery and performs an intracorporeal resection of the ileum using a flexible endostapler. Adhesions between the omentum, right colon, and anterior abdominal wall, as well as between the liver and proximal transverse colon, are divided. The hepatic flexure is mobilized from both superior and inferior approaches.
The surgeon overturns and suspends the specimen toward the right hypochondrium, achieving full mobilization of the right colon. The lower duodenal knee is visualized in the surgical field.

The surgeon performs colonic resection at the proximal transverse colon using a flexible endostapler, completing both ileal and colonic resections intracorporally. The dilated loops challenge the laparoscopic view, requiring skilled manipulation to maintain adequate working space. The freed specimen is placed in the right hypochondrium, and the distal ileal stump is retrieved. A mini incision is laparoscopically located over the colic stump for specimen extraction, followed by a proper mesocolic excision.

The surgeon performs an extracorporeal ileocolic anastomosis, hand-sewn in a side-to-side, isoparastaltic manner. The small bowel is carefully exteriorized to prevent massive peritoneal contamination due to severe distension, and then reintroduced into the abdominal cavity to ensure the correct posterior positioning of the anastomosis behind the dilated loops.
The surgeon laparoscopically inspects the anastomosis and checks for hemostasis after closing the mini-incision.
